does: This paper operationalizes pluralistic values in LLM alignment by collecting alignment data from 1,095 US and German participants rating model responses across five dimensions (toxicity, emotional awareness, sensitivity, stereotypical bias, and helpfulness). The authors fine-tune multiple LLMs and reasoning models using preferences from different demographic groups while systematically varying rating scales, disagreement handling methods, and optimization techniques. Key empirical findings reveal substantial demographic effects in preference annotations: male participants rate responses 18% less toxic than female participants, and conservative and Black participants rate responses 27.9% and 44% higher on emotional awareness compared to liberal and White participants respectively. These results expose how standard alignment procedures may encode majority preferences while marginalizing minority perspectives.
connects: This work extends the human feedback alignment paradigm pioneered in RLHF and related techniques by introducing demographic stratification as a central concern. It responds to growing recognition that alignment feedback is not value-neutral but reflects the annotators who provide it, building on prior work examining demographic biases in crowdsourced annotations and fairness in AI systems. The paper connects to emerging research on value pluralism in AI, questions about whose values get encoded in aligned models, and technical work on handling disagreement in preference learning. It addresses limitations of treating human feedback as monolithic and engages with sociological and fairness literatures questioning whose preferences dominate AI development.
enables: This work opens investigation into how to design alignment procedures that surface rather than obscure value disagreement across populations. It enables future research on multi-objective alignment mechanisms that explicitly optimize for inclusivity alongside safety, methods for aggregating genuinely diverse preferences without enforcing false consensus, and techniques for identifying and mitigating majority-preference dominance in model training. The systematic documentation of demographic effects supports building alignment systems that acknowledge legitimate value pluralism rather than treating diversity as a bug. This could lead toward LLMs trained to recognize and respect value disagreement, tools for communities to steer models toward their own values, and frameworks for understanding alignment as inherently political rather than technically neutral.
